intro:
The sea (Hebrew yam) and the deep (Hebrew tehôm; Greek abyssos) name the vast, restless waters that the ancient world feared as the realm of chaos, danger, and death. In Scripture the sea is more than scenery: it is the great untamed power that only the Creator can bound and master. The motif charts a single, hope-laden movement — from the chaotic deep over which the Spirit first hovered, to the new creation in which "the sea was no more."

At creation the earth lies formless beneath the deep until God's Spirit hovers over the waters and his word divides them, gathering the sea into its appointed place (Genesis 1:2; Genesis 1:9–10). The sea is good, yet held under firm limits: God shuts it behind doors and says, "Thus far shall you come, and no farther" (Job 38:8–11; Proverbs 8:29). When humanity's wickedness fills the earth, those limits are loosed in judgment and "the fountains of the great deep" burst forth in the flood (Genesis 7:11).

Israel's founding salvation is a victory over the sea. The LORD drives back the waters of the Red Sea so his people pass through on dry ground, then closes them over Pharaoh's army (Exodus 14:21–22; Exodus 15:8). The poets and prophets remember this as the LORD's mastery over chaos itself, often picturing the sea as a defeated monster — Rahab or Leviathan — whom God crushes and rules (Psalm 74:13–14; Psalm 89:9; Isaiah 51:9–10). To still the roaring sea is the LORD's own prerogative (Psalm 65:7); to be cast into its depths, as Jonah was, is to taste death and yet be raised by his mercy (Jonah 2:3–6).

In the Gospels Jesus exercises this divine authority directly. He rebukes the wind and waves so that the storm falls dead calm, leaving his disciples to ask, "Who then is this, that even the wind and the sea obey him?" (Mark 4:39–41). He walks upon the sea, treading the chaos beneath his feet as only God does (Job 9:8; Matthew 14:25). The deep that swallowed Jonah becomes his own sign of death and resurrection (Matthew 12:40): the Lord goes down into the depths and rises, conquering the last enemy.

The motif resolves in the new creation. In Revelation the wicked world-powers rise out of the sea (Revelation 13:1), and the abyss is the prison of the powers of darkness (Revelation 20:1–3). But when heaven and earth are made new, John sees that "the sea was no more" (Revelation 21:1) — chaos, threat, and death finally and forever abolished. The sea matters because it measures the scope of God's saving power: the Creator who bounded the deep, parted it for his people, and trod it underfoot in Christ will at last banish it altogether, leaving a world of unbroken peace.
